name: timeconvert

metadata.name: TimeConvert
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules

documentation:
# TimeConvert
TimeConvert is a simple time convert script(library) for Python, built for human beings.

[![Gitter](https://badges.gitter.im/Brightcells/TimeConvert.svg)](https://gitter.im/Brightcells/TimeConvert?utm_source=badge&utm_medium=badge&utm_campaign=pr-badge&utm_content=body_badge)

# Installation
```shell
pip install TimeConvert
```

# Usage
```python
from TimeConvert import TimeConvert as tc

tc.utc_timestamp()
```

# Comparison
| Function Points | TimeConvert | Other |
| ---- | ---- | ---- |
| TimeStamp | tc.local_timestamp(ms=True) | time.time() |
| ISOFormat | tc.local_isostring() | datetime.datetime.now().isoformat() |


# Variable
```python
# Default Asia/Shanghai & %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S
from TimeConvert import TIME_ZONE, TIME_FORMAT

# Deassign TIME_ZONE & TIME_FORMAT
tc.__init__(timezone='Asia/Shanghai', format='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
```

# Reference
* isoweek.py - https://github.com/gisle/isoweek
* month.py - https://github.com/kstark/months

classes.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.__new__
documentation:
Initialize a Week tuple with the given year and week number.

The week number does not have to be within range.  The numbers
will be normalized if not.  The year must be within the range
1 to 9999.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.thisweek
documentation: Return the current week (local time).

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.fromordinal
documentation:
Return the week corresponding to the proleptic Gregorian ordinal,
where January 1 of year 1 starts the week with ordinal 1.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.fromstring
documentation: Return a week initialized from an ISO formatted string like "2011W08" or "2011-W08".

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.withdate
documentation: Return the week that contains the given datetime.date

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.weeks_of_year
documentation:
Return an iterator over the weeks of the given year.
Years have either 52 or 53 weeks.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.last_week_of_year
documentation:
Return the last week of the given year.
This week with either have week-number 52 or 53.

This will be the same as Week(year+1, 0), but will even work for
year 9999 where this expression would overflow.

The first week of a given year is simply Week(year, 1), so there
is no dedicated classmethod for that.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.day
documentation: Return the given day of week as a date object.  Day 0 is the Monday.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.__add__
documentation:
Adding integers to a Week gives the week that many number of weeks into the future.
Adding with datetime.timedelta is also supported.

methods.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.__sub__
documentation:
Subtracting two weeks give the number of weeks between them as an integer.
Subtracting an integer gives another Week in the past.

class_variables.name: TimeConvert.isoweek.Week.isoformat
documentation: compatibility with datetime.date

A Week represents a period of 7 days starting with a Monday.
Weeks are identified by a year and week number within the year.
This corresponds to the read-only attributes 'year' and 'week'.

Week 1 of a year is defined to be the first week with 4 or more days in
January.  The preceeding week is either week 52 or 53 of the
preceeding year.

Week objects are tuples, and thus immutable, with an interface
similar to the standard datetime.date class.

methods.name: TimeConvert.stopwatch.StopWatch.__init__
documentation:
stop_watch计时器
**线程不安全** 请在使用时 实例化新对象进行计时
:param unique_id: 计时器的唯一标识，一般起比较有特点的名称
:param keep_task_list: 是否保存单个计时点(计时任务)的信息
:param time_type: 计时器输出的时间单位 默认毫秒
eg:
    sw = StopWatch('计时器唯一名称', time_type=TimeType.second)
    sw.start('我是计时点1')
    time.sleep(1)
    # 计时点1计时结束
    sw.stop()

    sw.start('我是计时点2')
    time.sleep(2)
    # 计时点2计时结束
    sw.stop()
    print(sw.pretty_print())

output:
    stopWatch [我是一个计时器]: running time = 3.0048 s
    ---------------------------------------------
    s             %         Task name
    ---------------------------------------------
    0000001.0043  033.42%  我是计时点1
    0000002.0005  066.58%  我是计时点2

methods.name: TimeConvert.stopwatch.StopWatch.start
documentation:
开始计时
\:param task_name: 计时任务(计时点)的名称
\:return

modules.name: TimeConvert.month
documentation:
Class file for a Month object.
Provides various utilities for generating, manipulating, and displaying
months.
import months
\>>> month = months.Month(2015, 4)
\>>> print(month.full_display)
'April 2015'
\>>> print(month.month_abbr)
'Apr'
\>>> print(month + 9)
'2016-01'
\>>> print(month.start_date)
datetime.date(2015, 4, 1)
\>>> print(month.n_days)
30
\>>> print(month.dates[-1])
datetime.date(2015, 4, 30)
\>>> print(month.nth(-1))
datetime.date(2015, 4, 30)
\>>> print(month.to(2015, 5))
[Month(2015, 4), Month(2015, 5)]
\>>> print(month.distance(month + 3))
3
\>>> print(month.gregorian_month_number)
24172
\>>> print(int(month))
201504
\>>> print(float(month))
201504.0

functions.name: TimeConvert.month.__utctoday
documentation: Return today's date in UTC time.

functions.name: TimeConvert.month._get
documentation:
Coerce an arbitrary object into a Month type.
This is designed to be used in functions accepting arbitrary type
arguments in an args situation, in which the value is expected to be
comparable to a Month.
Accepted types:
1\. Month
2\. Date / Datetime
3\. Two-value tuples (in args or as a single argument).
4\. Single-value lists/tuples containing one of the above.
\>>> \_\_get((2018, 1))
Month(2018, 1)

functions.name: TimeConvert.month._handle_other_decorator
documentation:
Decorate functions to handle "other" Month-like arguments.
The arguments are assumed to be within *args, while only a single
value (the Month) is actually desired for the function's execution.
The __get function coerces the value to a month and passes it to the
decorated function.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.n_days
documentation:
Return the number of days in the month.
\>>> Month(2018, 1).n_days
31

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.gregorian_month_number
documentation:
Return the number of months since the start of Gregorian year 1.
Year 0 and month 0 are invalid. So the first month of year 1 is 1, and
the first month of year -1 is -1.
\>>> Month(1, 1).gregorian_month_number
1
\>>> Month(2, 2).gregorian_month_number
14
\>>> Month(-1, 2).gregorian_month_number
-2

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.dates
documentation:
Return a tuple of all days in the month.
\>>> Month(2018, 1).dates[:2]
(datetime.date(2018, 1, 1), datetime.date(2018, 1, 2))

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.from_date
documentation:
Return a Month instance from given a date or datetime object.
Parameters
----------
date : date or datetime
    A date/datetime object as implemented via the standard lib module.
Returns
-------
month : Month
    The month object for that date.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.from_today
documentation: Return a Month instance from today's date (local time).

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.from_utc_today
documentation: Return a Month instance from today's date (UTC time).

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.__add__
documentation:
Offset a number of months into the future.
\>>> Month(2015, 4) + 9
Month(2016, 1)
Parameters
\----------
other : int

> Integer number of months to add.

# Returns

month

: The month object offset N months.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.__sub__
documentation:
Offset a number of months into the past.
\>>> Month(2015, 4) - 9
Month(2014, 7)
Parameters
\----------
other : int

> Integer number of months to subtract.

# Returns

month

: The month object offset -N months.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.start_date
documentation: Return a datetime.date object for the first day of the month.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.end_date
documentation: Return a datetime.date object for the last day of the month.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.range
documentation: Return a tuple of the first and last days of the month.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.nth
documentation:
Get date object for nth day of month.
Accepts nonzero integer values between +- `month.n_days`.
\>>> Month(2018, 1).nth(1) == Month(2018, 1).start_date
True
\>>> Month(2018, 1).nth(8)
datetime.date(2018, 1, 8)
\>>> Month(2018, 1).nth(-2)
datetime.date(2018, 1, 30)
Parameters
\----------
day : int

> Day of the month.

# Returns

date

: Date object for the day of the month.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.to
documentation:
Generate a list of all months between two months, inclusively.
Accepts two-element lists/tuples, date-like objects, or Month objects.
If months are provided out of order (like `june_18.to.march_18`) then
the list will also be in reverse order.
\>>> Month(2018, 1).to(Month(2018, 2))
[Month(2018, 1), Month(2018, 2)]
\>>> Month(2018, 3).to(2018, 1)
[Month(2018, 3), Month(2018, 2), Month(2018, 1)]
Parameters
\----------
other : Month, date, datetime, tuple

> A Month-like object.

# Returns

months

: List of months spanning the two objects, inclusively.

methods.name: TimeConvert.month.Month.distance
documentation:
Return the number of months distance between months.
This will always be a positive number. Accepts two-element lists/tuples
or Month objects.
\>>> Month(2018, 1).distance(Month(2018, 12))
11
\>>> Month(2018, 5).distance(2018, 1)
4
Parameters
\----------
other : Month, date, datetime, tuple

> A Month-like object.

# Returns

n_months

: Integer number of months distance.

Represent a specific month of a year.
Provides various utilities for generating, manipulating, and displaying
months.

documentation: TIMESTAMP ==> DATETIME
local_stamp => utc_datetime - fromtimestamp + to_utc_datetime / utcfromtimestamp
local_stamp => local_datetime - fromtimestamp
utc_stamp => utc_datetime - fromtimestamp
utc_stamp => local_datetime - fromtimestamp + to_local_datetime

methods.name: TimeConvert.convert.TimeConvertTools.is_aware
documentation:
Determine if a given datetime.datetime is aware.
The concept is defined in Python's docs:
https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo
Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,
value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.
AWARE vs. NAIVE
By design, these four functions don't perform any checks on their arguments.
The caller should ensure that they don't receive an invalid value like None.

methods.name: TimeConvert.convert.TimeConvertTools.is_naive
documentation:
Determine if a given datetime.datetime is naive.
The concept is defined in Python's docs:
https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo
Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,
value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.
